title: Semantics for Rational Consequence - Ranked Preferential Models

charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of ranked preferential models, the consequence relation they generate, and the representation theorem connecting semantics with the GM rules.

nodes.label:
Week 4
Ranked Models
nodes.note: Week 4 introduces a semantic counterpart to the GM proof system: ranked preferential models. The Representation Theorem shows that this semantics exactly captures the rational consequence relations.

nodes.label:
Ranked Preferential
Model
nodes.note: A finite ordered sequence of sets of valuations S1,...,Sk, where S1 contains the most usual worlds and Sk the most exceptional. Some valuations may be left out as impossible.

nodes.label:
Associated
Consequence Relation
nodes.note: F approxS G holds iff the minimal layer Sj intersecting mod(F) has every such valuation also satisfying G. Vacuously true if no layer contains any F-world.

nodes.label: Theorem 2.3
nodes.note: Every relation approxS generated by a ranked preferential model is rational - it satisfies all seven Gabbay-Makinson rules. Proved by case analysis on each rule.

nodes.label:
Theorem 2.4
(Representation)
nodes.note: Every rational consequence relation arises from some ranked preferential model. Combined with Theorem 2.3 this means rational = rankedly representable.

nodes.label:
Derived
Rules
nodes.note: The Representation Theorem provides a powerful proof technique: prove rules like SC, CON, CC and NRAT by checking they hold in every ranked model.

nodes.label:
Counter-model
Method
nodes.note: To show a conclusion is NOT derivable from premises by the GM rules, construct one ranked preferential model satisfying all premises but failing the conclusion.

nodes.label:
Preferential vs
Ranked
nodes.note: If we allow a partial order instead of a total linear order over layers we get preferential models, which satisfy every GM rule except Rational Monotonicity (RMON).

nodes.label: Definition 2.2
nodes.note: The formal definition of approxS uses set-theoretic operations on mod(F) to find the minimal layer with non-empty F-intersection, then checks containment in mod(G).

nodes.label:
Vacuous
Case
nodes.note: If no layer contains any model of F at all, then F approxS G holds for every G. F is treated as impossible in the model.

nodes.label:
Why RMON
fails partial
nodes.note: RMON requires the minimal layer to be uniquely determined. With a partial order, multiple minimal incomparable layers can exist, breaking the rational monotonicity argument.

nodes.label:
Verifying
Each GM Rule
nodes.note: Theorem 2.3's proof checks REF, R-and, L-or, LLE, RWE, CMON and RMON one at a time, using set-theoretic identities like mod(F-and-G) = mod(F) intersect mod(G).

nodes.label:
Constructing
the Model
nodes.note: Theorem 2.4's proof builds the model layer by layer: S1 contains the worlds satisfying every F such that True snake F, then S2 is built from the remaining worlds, and so on.

nodes.label:
Classical
Consequence
nodes.note: Classical consequence is itself rational: take a single layer S1 containing every possible valuation. This gives F entails G iff F approxS G.

charts.title: Evaluating F approxS G (Definition 2.2)
charts.description: Procedure for deciding whether a non-monotonic consequence holds in a given ranked preferential model.

nodes.label:
Given S = (S1,...,Sk)
and check F snake G
nodes.note: We are given a ranked preferential model and want to test whether the non-monotonic consequence F approxS G holds according to Definition 2.2.

nodes.label: Set i = 1
nodes.note: Initialise the layer index. We will scan layers from the most usual S1 outwards, looking for the first one that contains any model of F.

nodes.label: i <= k ?
nodes.note: Check whether more layers remain to scan. If we exhaust all layers without finding any model of F, the relation holds vacuously.

nodes.label:
mod(F) cap Si
empty?
nodes.note: Test whether layer Si contains any propositional valuation that satisfies F. The first layer where this is non-empty becomes the minimal layer.

nodes.label: i = i + 1
nodes.note: No model of F was found in this layer. Move on to the next less typical layer and repeat the check.

nodes.label:
Vacuously true:
F snake G holds
nodes.note: No layer contains any model of F. By the second clause of Definition 2.2, F approxS G holds automatically - F is treated as impossible.

nodes.label:
mod(F) cap Si
subset of mod(G)?
nodes.note: We have found the minimal layer Sj intersecting mod(F). Now check whether every valuation in this intersection also satisfies G.

nodes.label: F snake G holds
nodes.note: Every model of F in the minimal layer is also a model of G. The non-monotonic consequence F approxS G holds in this model.

nodes.label: F does NOT snake G
nodes.note: Some valuation in mod(F) cap Sj fails G. The non-monotonic consequence does not hold and the model serves as a counter-example.

charts.title: Counter-model Method
charts.description: How to show that a conclusion cannot be derived from a set of premises by constructing a ranked preferential counter-model.

nodes.label:
Premises P1...Pn
and candidate conclusion C
nodes.note: We want to show that the conclusion C cannot be derived from premises P1,...,Pn using only the GM rules. The Representation Theorem makes this provable by counter-model.

nodes.label:
Design layers S1, S2, ...
listing valuations
nodes.note: Choose a small ranked preferential model. Pick which propositional valuations go into each layer. Aim for a minimal set of layers that just covers the relevant cases.

nodes.label:
Check premises
hold in S
nodes.note: For each premise, evaluate it using Definition 2.2. Verify that every premise is satisfied in the candidate model.

nodes.label:
All premises
hold?
nodes.note: If any premise fails, the model is invalid as a counter-model. Adjust the layers and recheck. If they all hold, continue to the conclusion.

nodes.label:
Adjust layers
or valuations
nodes.note: Modify the ranked model so that the failing premise becomes true. This may involve adding, removing or reordering valuations across layers.

nodes.label:
Check conclusion C
in S
nodes.note: Now evaluate the candidate conclusion in the model. We want it to fail so that the model is a counter-example to derivability.

nodes.label: Conclusion fails?
nodes.note: Test whether C fails in the model. If C fails, we have a counter-model and the proof is complete; if it holds, we need a different counter-model.

nodes.label:
Adjust layers to
make C fail
nodes.note: If the candidate model satisfies C, modify the layers (still keeping the premises true) until the conclusion is falsified by some valuation in the relevant minimal layer.

nodes.label:
Counter-model proves
C NOT derivable
nodes.note: The model satisfies every premise but falsifies C. By the Representation Theorem, C cannot be derived from the premises using the GM rules.

charts.title: Verifying GM Rules in a Ranked Model (Theorem 2.3)
charts.description: Classification of the seven Gabbay-Makinson rules and the key argument used to verify each one in any ranked preferential model.

nodes.label:
Theorem 2.3:
approxS is rational
nodes.note: We must show that the consequence relation generated by any ranked model satisfies every GM rule. Each rule has a short set-theoretic proof.

nodes.label: REF
nodes.note: Reflexivity F snake F: at the minimal layer Sj, mod(F) cap Sj is trivially a subset of mod(F).

nodes.label: R-and
nodes.note: If both G and H hold at the minimal layer for F, then so does their intersection mod(G) cap mod(H) = mod(G-and-H).

nodes.label: L-or
nodes.note: Use mod(F-or-G) = mod(F) union mod(G) and split the minimal layer for the disjunction into the two sub-cases.

nodes.label: LLE
nodes.note: If mod(F) = mod(G) classically, then the same layer with the same intersection works for both, so F snake H gives G snake H.

nodes.label: RWE
nodes.note: If mod(G) is a subset of mod(H), then the inclusion at the minimal layer extends from mod(G) to mod(H).

nodes.label: CMON
nodes.note: The minimal layer for F-and-G is at least as deep as that for F. Containment in mod(H) follows from containment of the F intersection.

nodes.label: RMON
nodes.note: Since F does NOT snake not-G, no earlier layer can wipe out G in the F intersection. So the minimal layer for F-and-G coincides with that for F.

nodes.label:
Key tool: minimal
layer Sj
nodes.note: Every proof revolves around the same idea: identify the minimal layer Sj where mod(F) cap Sj is non-empty, then reason about set inclusions in that layer.

charts.title: Constructing a Ranked Model from a Rational Relation (Theorem 2.4)
charts.description: Recursive construction used in the proof of the Representation Theorem to recover a ranked model from any rational consequence relation.

nodes.label:
Given rational
relation snake
nodes.note: We are given a rational consequence relation snake and want to construct a ranked preferential model whose induced relation equals snake.

nodes.label:
Set S1 = intersect
of mod(F) for True snake F
nodes.note: The most usual worlds are those satisfying every F such that True snake F. Take S1 to be the intersection of mod(F) over all such F.

nodes.label: Set i = 1
nodes.note: Initialise the layer index. We have constructed S1 and will now build the next layer recursively.

nodes.label:
S(i+1) non-empty
possible?
nodes.note: Check whether there are any valuations left outside S1 union ... union Si. If not, the construction terminates here.

nodes.label:
Pick Theta_i with
mod(Theta_i) = complement
of S1...Si
nodes.note: Select a formula whose models are exactly the valuations not yet placed into a layer. This formula represents the next less typical case.

nodes.label:
S(i+1) = intersect
of mod(F) for Theta_i snake F
nodes.note: The next layer collects the worlds satisfying every F such that Theta_i snake F. These are the most usual worlds among those still unplaced.

nodes.label: i = i + 1
nodes.note: Increment the index and continue building layers. Because the universe is finite the construction must terminate after finitely many steps.

nodes.label: Output S = (S1,...,Sk)
nodes.note: Take the finite sequence of constructed layers as the ranked preferential model. By the correctness proof, its induced relation equals snake.
